channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Clutch Is the Fast & Furious Video Game I've Always Wanted
item.content:encoded:
In the world of racing games, Forza Horizon is the holy grail that delightfully threads that needle between hardcore simulation and easygoing arcade-style gameplay. Pair that with endless roads to burn rubber on and you've got a widely appealing game that carries Burnout Paradise's torch.

I never found myself wanting a story from these games; after all, if we're being honest, the narrative doesn't hold much dramatic weight. It's often an excuse to race against hot air balloons and fighter jets for a crowd of music and motor fest junkies.

Then I saw Clutch at Summer Game Fest. The team at Maverick Games walked me through their vision of the game and the first couple missions to set the scene. Will it tell a story that stands the test of video game time? It's hard to say, but it's already gears ahead of any other racing game I've seen (though to be fair, I haven't played Screamer).

A Legacy in Racing

I brought up Forza Horizon earlier primarily because of the team behind Clutch. Maverick Games, a new studio in the U.K., was founded by leadership from Playground Games. That pedigree is apparent when you see Clutch in motion. The mini map UI looks similar, and there's something about the way it moves that feels familiar.

"As is at the moment, I try not to reference my previous games too much, but it's not a million miles away from that. We simulate the weight of the car more so than you might expect," said Mike Brown, founder and creative director at Maverick. "At the moment, the assist setup for each car is kind of similar to what you'd expect on the real-life version of that car. But that will be player tunable."

Clutch features many real-life cars in it, which reflects their company culture as a bunch of car enthusiasts. Brown joked that if you join the team without a driver's license, you're likely to end up purchasing an enthusiast car at some point.

You'll go between professional, official motorsport races and underground circuits run by the Midnight Collective. The professional races play as you'd expect: win by placing first after running some laps around the track.

The underground races, however, are scored very differently. Each car streams their performance live, and chat will ask the player to do certain tricks, like drift. Doing so will get you more viewers, as does passing through each checkpoint on the track. At the end, you're awarded more viewers based on your place in the race (obviously, first place gets the most viewers). The racer with the most viewers wins.

This puts a unique spin on the entire genre; in the hands-off preview we watched, the player got first place but just barely lost to the second-place racer because they got more viewers overall. You have to drive well and stylishly to win these underground races, and there's more strategy involved since you can skip checkpoints however you please. Taking a shortcut may ensure you get first place, but are you doing enough tricks to make up for the lost viewers?

Spinning Up a Narrative Hook

But there's more that Maverick is bringing to set Clutch apart. For one, there's a full-on story starring two orphaned siblings: Theo (played by Tosin Cole) and Cass Martial (played by Little Simz). The demo opens on them in the back of a social worker's Fiat Multipla. Theo's being scolded and threatened with never finding a foster parent if he continues to act out. He responds, of course, by taking the wheel when the worker leaves the car, driving at the tender (and illegal) age of 11.

Then the game fast forwards to the future, ahead of where the game starts off. We're in media res as Theo's being chased by the cops on a scenic highway. It's frantic, hectic, and the definition of high octane. There are a few more teases of future events, including a scene where Cass does a probably illegal favor for Theo in a Jeep.

Once we jump into present day, we see Theo and Cass at the Riviera 1K, a legendary race in the world of Clutch. Theo's the underdog to win this year, but the race gets interrupted after his rival Miko dies in a horrific crash that kills and injures a few more racers.

This event presents all sorts of conflicts for Theo. The commission behind the Riviera 1K is threatening to change regulations to make racing safer, which upsets Theo's foster father. Theo himself loses faith in the Riviera 1K and questions his motivation as a motorsport athlete.

Along the way, he steals a car from a powerful person in the racing world, forcing him to break more rules and owe people more favors just to preserve his place. In the final mission we saw, he had to sneak into a ritzy apartment and steal an Aston Martin Valhalla. To get in, Theo has to dodge a patrol car, which you can do by parking, turning off the engine, and ducking so no one could see a person in the car.

Upon stealing the Valhalla, you find out that the real reason you're stealing the car is the Clutchtech within. It has a grappling hook on it—the very same you see at the end of the trailer that shoots into a helicopter, letting Theo gently (ish) swing from the top floor to ground level.

In gameplay, this hook lets you slingshot around really tight switchbacks, attack enemy cars, or pull yourself toward a goal—like into the back of a giant truck moving over 100 miles per hour.

Obviously, there's a lot more to the story here that Maverick hasn't shown us yet. Brown told us that as the story unfolds, the radio DJs will react to the events of the narrative, which reminded me a bit of Grand Theft Auto. Other times, they might just share some fun historical facts about cars.

Brown also promised that while we mostly saw the preview through Theo's eyes, Cass is also playable. Furthermore, people will fall in love with her and her story as she moves outside of her older brother's shadow.

Shifting Into Morocco and Beyond

Clutch will feature three maps to explore: Monaco, Saint-Tropez, and a fictional mishmash of French cities called Corrine. We mostly saw Monaco, which Brown told us is even larger than the real-life city.

"Monaco is a very, very dense place. We've allowed our level designers to add a little room where it was needed to make it a little bit more drivable," Brown said. "Real-life Monaco, much like our rendering of it, is also a really layered city. So you've got network of tunnels underground. You've got a street level thing. There are elevated overpasses as well. Genuinely one of the most complex cities ever built in a video game, but really fun to explore and learn."

And there's a lot to explore in Monaco alone, based on what I saw. There are collectibles off the beaten path, cool stunts to discover, and almost 800 roads to discover (yes, Clutch keeps track of roads the same way Forza Horizon does). There are landmarks from real-life Monaco to see in the game too, so it'll be cool to see how Maverick renders them in the game.

Everything we saw looked absolutely stunning. Racing games have always been at the forefront of graphical fidelity, especially when it comes to rendering photorealism. That remains true here; every vista looked breathtaking, and even the little details in the areas you could walk around look beautifully realized.

I might be a little biased as a fan of Forza Horizon, but I think Maverick has something really special with Clutch. The racing gameplay looks promising, and it's always thrilling to blitz down a highway or fly off a makeshift ramp into the sunset. With a focus on narrative that could appeal to a wider audience, this could be a big hit for the team, and in a world where Horizon has few competitors, I gladly welcome this newcomer.

Clutch is slated for a 2027 release on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S.

Clutch was seen during a hands-off preview at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/clutch-is-fast-furious-video-game-ive-always-wanted
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Stranger Than Heaven's Combat Puts Extra Weight, Thought, and Style Into Every Hit
item.content:encoded:
Stranger Than Heaven is getting closer to living up to its name. With every new reveal trailer, I have more questions than answers, but maybe that's the point. After all, now I'm enticed to play the game for myself to get all the answers—though to be honest, as a big Like a Dragon fan, I was going to play this one anyway.

During Summer Game Fest Play Days, I got my hands on the next Ryu Ga Gotoku game. I still don't understand why Tupac is in the game or what roles he and Snoop Dogg will play, but I at least have some answers related to how the combat feels.

To put it simply, it's gritty, deliberate, and stylish. There's a lot of room to express yourself in response to enemy attacks since you can control the left and right sides of Makoto Daito somewhat separately. This system adds just enough strategy to every moment of combat that makes it feel like a total evolution from previous Like a Dragon games.

To the Left, to the Right

I had roughly 35 minutes with the Stranger Than Heaven demo, and it featured three snippets of combat, each increasing in difficulty. The first two were fights against a group of enemies, while the final fight was with a single ronin-like character who was tricky and deadly.

If you missed the gameplay deep dive trailer, the controls are drastically different compared to the studio's Yakuza titles. The left shoulder buttons control Makoto's left limbs, depending on the context. The trigger will do heavier attacks. The equal and opposite is true for the right shoulder buttons, and each button in a combo string can be held down to charge up their effect.

For stringing together combos, it's less about hitting "light, light, heavy," but more about considering the flow of Makoto's body as he attacks. For example, with his fists, doing a right-left-right-left string moves pretty naturally, but you can also change things up as he'll follow up a left punch with a left kick.

On the other hand, when he wields a knife, alternating between the light and heavy right-side attacks will absolutely cut up some baddies. However, this is where your sense of style comes into play. You can still attack with the left side of his body, flowing in punches or kicks to build a combo that alternates between blunt and sharp attacks.

The math totally changes with the two-handed crowbar. The buttons now determine which direction you swing as opposed to which limb you activate. It's a subtle difference, but it makes sense when you're trying to hit as many enemies as possible depending on where they are.

That directional focus also comes into play for blocking and parrying. You can hold down the block button to mitigate damage at the cost of stamina, but if you block in the direction of an enemy attack, it'll increase how much damage you block and decrease the stamina cost.

If you block in the right direction with perfect timing, you'll parry the enemy, slowing down time and opening them up for a big counterattack. The same happens when you do a perfect dodge, so having great reaction timing can really open up enemies for major damage.

Holding down the triggers initiates a grab action, which, like most other attacks, depends on the context. When you have your fists equipped, you'll tackle an enemy, and if their stamina is low enough, they'll go down while you get on top of them. If you don't bring them down, you can struggle against them and even push them away depending on which direction you press.

With the knife equipped, both triggers would execute a command grab. If it successfully connects, you'll watch a canned animation of Makoto stabbing the enemy and pushing them away.

I feel like I've said it a lot, but context really is the driving factor in the combat for Stranger Than Heaven. It impacts what combos you can do, what finishers you can execute, and what reactions you have to take.

You have so many options that you can act, react, and smack down enemies with ruthless panache.

For example, in the second fight, I had to take down a handful of normal dudes and one giant beefy boy with a sword. The challenge here is that the beefy boy can charge into you from pretty much wherever, and whenever you try to grab him, he grabs you back and throws you instead. His attacks cover a wide range and hit pretty hard, too.

So you never want to sit still for too long—and the enemies know that. The little guys will try to grapple you; if they come from behind, they'll put you in a nearly full nelson, and you have to struggle out of it by mashing the attack buttons.

However, when an enemy grabs you from the side, they'll hold onto one of your arms. As you might expect, mashing the buttons on that side will make Makoto struggle for freedom. On the other side, he can use his free arm to attack his grappler—or even other enemies who approach. Time it right, and you can grab an enemy by the head and smash him into your grappler.

You might have seen that move depicted in one of the gameplay trailers, like I did. I wondered how hands-on that move would be, and it turns out that it's something that you the player can deliberately do.

The final fight against the ronin is where everything really clicked for me. He felt like a skill-check boss in a Like a Dragon game, where you realize you can't just button mash your way through. Plus, he's got devastating attacks, especially one command grab that can wipe out ¾ of your health.

The difficulty forced me to slow down and focus on reacting. I could really appreciate the directional combat here; reading which direction he attacked from let me parry his terrifying katana with my little knife, which was so rewarding every single time.

However, you don't want to play purely reactionary here. It's all about finding that balance between aggression and defense. There's a certain stance the ronin takes where if you're aggressive, he'll unleash a big combo. But if you sit back and wait too long, he'll heal up. It's all one big game of "if this, then that," but your options for "that" are wide open.

All in all, the combat in Stranger Than Heaven is immensely satisfying. It's slower and more deliberate than previous real-time action Like a Dragon games. You have so many options that you can act, react, and smack down enemies with ruthless panache.

You can pick up Stranger Than Heaven on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S when it launches on January 15, 2027.

Stranger Than Heaven was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/stranger-than-heavens-combat-puts-extra-weight-thought-and-style-into-every-hit
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Aniimo Seeks To Further Blend Live Service Exploration With Monster Battling And Collecting
item.content:encoded:
As a long-time fan of games where you collect, capture, or battle monsters, I've been intrigued by the premise of Aniimo since its announcement. Especially as we've seen other players in the Monster Catching meet Live Service Space.

In Aniimo, you play as a Pathfinder, a mix of scientist and researcher who explores the main island, doing what they can to explore the island of Aniimo, capturing them, and befriending them to be able to explore further and tackle different mysteries.

For my demo with Aniimo, I got set up with a high-level character, and plenty of Aniimo to join me on my adventure, and I was immediately overwhelmed with my choices.

Exploring my immediate surroundings, I was able to locate groups of roaming monsters. A neat aspect of these that while each would be roughly the same level, that there would normally be one evolution of the Aniimo, while a few smaller versions of it followed it around. It almost gave the vibe of a pack of wolves, or a duck and their ducklings.

For combat, I was able to get close and have my Aniimo fight them, or I could take over control of my Aniimo and fight directly. Combat felt very similar to other open-world free-to-play titles from the past decade or so. You've got quick and heavy attacks, as well as two to three different big abilities to pull out at a moments notice.

What was quite strange to me was that while all of my Aniimo were around the level 70 mark, fighting any of the level ~55 Aniimo around me was a pretty long process. There's a lot of sponge in those fights.

Part of me wonders whether for the build they have been scaled to a high level, but the kind of things you might have at your disposal to boost specific stats weren't implemented, leading to Aniimo that were high enough level to be safe to roam the world without being too overpowered.

You have four Aniimo at any time in your party and quickly swap between them, but you can also access the equivalent of your "Box" when you're freely roaming and not in combat.

Each Aniimo has its own elemental affinity that helps in combat, pairing strong elements against weak elements to get the upper hand. These elements are also used in puzzle solving in the overworld.

Two environmental puzzles that I encountered required specific elements in order to complete them. The first was a check trapped behind wooden brambles that I couldn't reach; what I was able to reach, was a wooden ball swinging in the wind on a chain. I was able to get a fire Aniimo to set the ball alight and watch it swing up into the brambles, freeing the chest.

The chest that I found levitating in the sky had a fire under it, causing an updraft. This puzzle was simpler to solve, but still required you to seek out and obtain a specific Aniimo or type of Aniimo to progress.

Aniimo Can Evolve, And Come In Different Colors

Just as you'd expect, the Aniimo that you find in the overworld are able to evolve. As I was perusing through the list of Aniimo I had available to me, I saw a number that evolved when you reached certain levels, but there were also a fair few whose evolution was tied to completing questlines.

I liked the idea of getting to a certain point with an Aniimo that was of a certain style and power, and then defeating a large boss, and suddenly a new level of power was unlocked. It felt like a more satisfying kind of progression than just "Higher level = Higher power."

On top of that, when looking through the record of Aniimo that were available, I found that there were Sparkling variants as well as Darkler variants. For the purpose of the demo, I wasn't able to see what this would involve, but if you're a fan of shiny hunting in Pokémon, then I think you might have a new addiction releasing soon.

Aniimo Feels Like A Safe Merge Of Very Powerful Features

Overall, stepping away from my time with Aniimo, the game felt somewhat safe in its approach to quest design, exploration, and combat but is leaning far more heavily into the fighting with your monsters instead of just travelling alongside them or using them to help craft the world around you.

I think Aniimo has a great chance to become very popular at launch and with upcoming network tests, but how it will continue to expand and iterate past launch will be what proves whether it's got the greatness in it or not.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/aniimo-seeks-to-further-blend-live-service-exploration-with-monster-battling-and
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Tomb Raider: Legacy Of Atlantis Brings Lara Back To Her Roots: Raiding, Shooting, And Dinosaurs
item.content:encoded:
I've been a huge fan of Lara Croft ever since her first adventure, though I did normally just go and play around at Croft Manor, and now in 2026, we're going to get to re-experience her first adventure (for the second time) on modern-day hardware. At Summer Game Fest, I had a chance to sit down and play almost an hour of Tomb Raider: Legacy of Atlantis, showing off some puzzle and exploration gameplay leading into the tomb of Qualopec and fierce fights against prehistoric foes.

Before getting started, we were greeted with a brief pre-recorded message by Jeff Adams, the Experience Director at Crystal Dynamics. It's been 30 years since Lara Croft made her debut appearance, so it was important to the team to imagine what scale that original adventure would have been, given the access to new technology.

Tomb Raider: Legacy of Atlantis seeks to capture the wonder of the original game, with new surprises that deepen the experience

Getting caught up on the story, we meet Lara in this demo after being hired by Jacqueline Natla to find the Scion of Atlantis. This relic is believed to have been split into three pieces, one of which is in a valley in Peru that's the home to the Tomb of Qualopec.

As the screen shifted into gameplay, I was met with Lara in the middle of a lush forest heading up a hill, with a strange structure coming out of a small body of water. Lara quickly points out that it's a way to stop a waterfall that's in our way, but that there are some gears missing from the mechanism in order to progress.

Immediately, I got to set out and explore the world. In the nearby vicinity, I found various plants for crafting, collectibles from the region, and a variety of out-of-reach handholds that I stored away in my mind to check out later.

Through this demo, I was required to locate two cog wheels to complete a mechanism to redirect the waterfall to open a new path forward. The first of these was easy to locate, using my grappling hook and a big tug to rip it off the wall over the water and drag it over to the rest of the mechanism.

Diving into the water to try to find the second cog wheel, I ended up instead locating a secret tunnel that came out to a cave with some new collectibles and a secret passage back to the hub area.

Collectibles that I found included historical artifacts that are themed after the area you collect them in. These are all set up in sets that already seem an early indicator to let you know if you're missing something in an area before moving too much further on. Another collectible that I ran into was the Tooth collectible. Obtaining two of these earned me a skill point that I could use to upgrade Lara's abilities. That menu was locked out for the demo, so I unfortunately wasn't able to actually see what Lara's skills could be.

I did end up finding three Teeth over the course of the demo, the first two giving me an ability point, while the third let me know that for my next upgrade point, I'd need four teeth instead. It's going to be important to select the upgrade you really want first if each subsequent upgrade is going to take just a little bit more collecting to obtain it.

To progress, I had to climb up the valley further, finding dilapidated temples overrun with vines and other foliage. This was more of your classic Tomb Raider fare, seeking out handholds in walls to get higher, while making some quick jumps over tricky platforms to progress. There were a few fantastic leaps of faith, forcing Lara to sprint and jump over a large gap, and some even had grapple points to turn your leap into a swing.

After reaching the top, knocking the second wheel out of place, and following it back down, the exploration section of the demo was over.

Tomb Raider Has Updated The Dinosaurs To Feature Feathers

Lara continued deeper into the valley, speaking to a feeling that she wasn't alone. It wasn't long before Lara got attacked by a group of dinosaurs ready for their next meal.

Pulling out dual pistols, I quickly got to work taking potshots at the raptor-like creatures. As my reticle moved between their body and heads as I was shooting, I could see slight indicators letting me know when I was doing extra damage.

I was incredibly impressed with the movement displayed by the raptors. The telegraphs for their attacks are easy to read but super satisfying and terrifying. Before one charges at you, you'll see it straighten up, and if it's about to make a flying leap, it will lower its body to prepare the momentum of flinging itself up and forward at you.

From what I could tell, Lara's pistols had unlimited ammo, but they did take an amount of time that was just long enough to start worrying about what was going on. It forced breaks in combat. Similarly, Lara's dodge ability, which had her performing cartwheels and tumbles, also seemed to let you do three in a row before there was a bit of a forced pause, leaving you helpless.

These were nice balances to combat that let you perform some very Lara Croft actions, but also still put an emphasis on being precise with your shots and not dodging too early that you get stuck.

The very first thing I did was try to pull out my guns and do a side flip, and unfortunately, Lara hopped to the side instead of the kinds of acrobatic motions I remembered from the original game. Just as I was starting to get disappointed about the lack of acrobatics in combat, I initiated combat focus, spending one of three charges on my UI, and Lara Croft performed a backflip/somersault/gainer… thing, and time slowed down.

There are moments in games where combat lets you do something that's just objectively cool, and the focus mode created for Tomb Raider: Legacy of Atlantis is one of them.

Combat felt stylish and entertaining as you'd feel proud about successful reads of enemy movements, nail the dinosaur in the hide with a few quick shots, and then, when you're just about to get attacked, you can flip out of it, scoring some easy headshots while the world slows around you.

The demo experience culminated in a running segment from a T. rex that was drawn to the action. This segment was primarily on rails, featuring Lara sliding through the mud to avoid the T. rex, performing quick clambers, and jumping onto collapsing objects as they tumbled from the force of the T. rex. There was a fantastic but brief moment where you're running towards the camera with the T. rex behind you that was clearly an homage to the dino-chase sequences from early Crash Bandicoot games.

With 30 years of enhancements in controls, graphics, design methodology, and more, it would be easy to think this was a completely new Lara Croft title. The focus on exploration and puzzles is working to ground it, while it still has that otherworldly/supernatural flair that the original Tomb Raider games had.

I can't wait to get my hands on the game when it releases in 2026, and I can't wait to figure out what I can use my upgrade points on.

Tomb Raider: Legacy of Atlantis was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/tomb-raider-legacy-of-atlantis-brings-lara-back-to-her-roots-raiding-shooting-and
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
